claim: U.S. guided-missile destroyers passed through the Strait of Hormuz for the first time since the war began on April 11-12, 2026.
The Israeli Air Force struck over 120 Hezbollah targets in southern Lebanon within the 24 hours prior to April 12, 2026.
Iran is fortifying the Isfahan nuclear facility with barriers, blocked roads, raised earthworks, and fencing to prepare for potential U.S. or Israeli ground raid.
U.S. intelligence assesses Iran retains over 1,000 medium-range ballistic missiles despite prior strikes, down from a pre-war stockpile of approximately 2,500.
Vice President JD Vance is leading a U.S. delegation to Pakistan on April 12, 2026, with envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner to negotiate ceasefire terms.
Trump stated that U.S. troops will remain in the Persian Gulf until full compliance with ceasefire terms and warned that 'the real shooting starts' if negotiations fail.
Iran and the U.S. disagree on negotiation frameworks, with Iran proposing a 10-point plan versus the U.S. 15-point plan.
Iran issued a reported 24-hour ultimatum to the U.S. before potentially walking away from ceasefire talks in Pakistan.
The Israeli IDF Division 98 is tightening a siege on Bent Jbeil and paratroopers are advancing to increase pressure on Hezbollah commanders.
Iran is issuing provocative messages during ceasefire negotiations in Pakistan as of April 11, 2026.
Lebanon's health ministry reported 182 killed in Israeli strikes in Lebanon, which Iran cited as violations of ceasefire terms.
U.S. Vice President Vance clarified that the ceasefire agreement excludes Lebanon-Hezbollah fighting.
Prior disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz caused global economic shocks with lingering effects on oil and gas prices.
Trump set an April 7, 2026 deadline for reopening the Strait of Hormuz as a final requirement, though economic damage was expected to persist.
Iran announced (then deleted) a delegation's arrival in Islamabad for weekend talks as of April 11, 2026.
